steps.title: Resolve the operator's local timezone
steps.intent: Determine the timezone in which the clock is rendered so that "local time" is meaningful for the operator.
steps.acceptance: The rendered wall-clock time equals the current time in the resolved timezone within 1 second of the request instant.
steps.stand_in: System/process local timezone (TZ) as a stand-in for an unspecified operator timezone source; the workload does not state where the timezone comes from.

steps.title: Format the current time as a clock
steps.intent: Produce a human-readable clock string from the current time in the resolved timezone.
steps.acceptance: The formatted clock string matches /^[0-2][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]$/ (24-hour HH:MM) for the resolved timezone.
steps.stand_in: 24-hour HH:MM format as a stand-in for an unspecified clock format (12h/24h, seconds, AM/PM not specified by the workload).

steps.title: Render the operator greeting with the clock
steps.intent: Combine a salutation with the formatted local clock into the operator-facing greeting.
steps.acceptance: The greeting response contains a salutation immediately followed by the formatted clock string produced by step-format-clock.
steps.stand_in: The "Hello, operator!" salutation from the frozen demo3 plan as a stand-in for unspecified greeting copy.

verification_targets.check: The rendered clock reflects the resolved local timezone, not UTC, when the two differ.
The greeting response contains both a salutation and an HH:MM clock string.

boundary_cases.description: When no operator timezone can be resolved, refuse with a setup hint instead of silently defaulting to UTC.
At 00:00 local time the clock renders "00:00" (never "24:00" or empty) and the greeting still reads naturally.
During a DST spring-forward or fall-back, the rendered local time reflects the UTC offset in effect at that instant.
